questions.stem: In the SIR model $\dot{S} = -\beta S I$, $\dot{I} = \beta S I - \gamma I$, $\dot{R} = \gamma I$, the compartments $S$, $I$, $R$ stand for
questions.explanation: SIR partitions the population into Susceptible (can catch the disease), Infectious (currently infected and transmitting), and Recovered or Removed (immune, no longer susceptible or infectious). The flows run $S \to I \to R$.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: In the SIR equations $\dot{S} = -\beta S I$, $\dot{I} = \beta S I - \gamma I$, the parameter $\gamma$ is the
questions.explanation: The term $\gamma I$ moves individuals from $I$ to $R$, so $\gamma$ is the per capita recovery (removal) rate. Its reciprocal $1/\gamma$ is the mean infectious period. The transmission rate is $\beta$.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: For the basic SIR model, the basic reproduction number is
questions.explanation: The basic reproduction number is the average number of secondary infections produced by one infectious individual in a wholly susceptible population. It equals the transmission rate times the mean infectious period: $R_0 = \beta \cdot \frac{1}{\gamma} = \frac{\beta}{\gamma}$.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: The mean infectious period in the SIR model (the average time an individual spends in compartment $I$) is
questions.explanation: Recovery is a constant-rate (exponential) process with rate $\gamma$, so the average residence time in $I$ is $1/\gamma$. This is why $R_0 = \beta/\gamma$ can be read as transmission rate $\times$ infectious period.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: Starting from an almost entirely susceptible population ($S_0 \approx 1$), an epidemic takes off (the number of infectives initially grows) if and only if
questions.explanation: At the start $\dot{I} = (\beta S_0 - \gamma) I \approx (\beta - \gamma) I$. The infectives grow when $\beta - \gamma > 0$, that is $\beta/\gamma > 1$, i.e. $R_0 > 1$. This is the threshold theorem: an epidemic occurs only above $R_0 = 1$.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: The threshold value of $R_0$ separating a dying-out infection from an epidemic is
questions.explanation: At $R_0 = 1$ each infective replaces itself with exactly one new infective on average, so the infection neither grows nor declines. Below $1$ it fades; above $1$ it spreads. Hence $R_0 = 1$ is the critical threshold.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: A disease has transmission rate $\beta = 0.6$ per day and recovery rate $\gamma = 0.2$ per day. Its basic reproduction number is
questions.explanation: $R_0 = \frac{\beta}{\gamma} = \frac{0.6}{0.2} = 3$. On average one infectious person generates three new infections in a fully susceptible population, so the disease will spread.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: The fraction of the population that must be immune to prevent an epidemic (the herd immunity threshold) is
questions.explanation: An epidemic is blocked once the susceptible fraction falls below $1/R_0$. The complementary fraction that must be immune is therefore $1 - 1/R_0$. Above this coverage, each case produces fewer than one secondary case and the infection cannot spread.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: Measles has $R_0 \approx 4$. The herd immunity threshold (minimum immune fraction to stop spread) is about
questions.explanation: The herd immunity threshold is $1 - \frac{1}{R_0} = 1 - \frac{1}{4} = 0.75$, i.e. 75% must be immune. (Real measles has $R_0 \approx 12$ to $18$, requiring well over 90%, but for $R_0 = 4$ the value is 75%.)
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: A disease has $R_0 = 5$. The minimum fraction that must be vaccinated to achieve herd immunity is
questions.explanation: $1 - \frac{1}{R_0} = 1 - \frac{1}{5} = 1 - 0.2 = 0.8$. So 80% of the population must be immune for the susceptible fraction to drop below the $1/R_0 = 0.2$ threshold.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: In the SIR model, the number of infectives $I(t)$ reaches its maximum (the epidemic peak) at the moment when
questions.explanation: The peak occurs when $\dot{I} = 0$ with $I \neq 0$: $\beta S I - \gamma I = 0$ gives $\beta S = \gamma$, so $S = \gamma/\beta = 1/R_0$. As $S$ falls through this value the epidemic turns over and infectives begin to decline.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: For an SIR epidemic with $R_0 = 4$, the susceptible fraction at the peak of the epidemic is
questions.explanation: The peak is where $S = 1/R_0 = 1/4 = 0.25$. Up to this point new infections outpace recoveries; once $S$ drops below $0.25$, recoveries dominate and $I$ declines.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: A key conclusion of the Kermack-McKendrick threshold theorem about how an epidemic ends is that
options: the epidemic stops only when every individual has been infected
the epidemic stops while some susceptibles still remain uninfected
the number of infectives grows without bound
the susceptible population returns to its initial value
questions.explanation: The epidemic dies out because $S$ falls below the threshold $1/R_0$ (so $\dot{I} < 0$) while $I$ is still positive but declining. It burns out before exhausting the susceptibles, so a positive fraction $S_\infty > 0$ escapes infection entirely.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: In the SIS model $\dot{I} = \beta S I - \gamma I$ with $S = 1 - I$ (no immunity), recovered individuals
options: return immediately to the susceptible class
questions.explanation: SIS stands for Susceptible-Infectious-Susceptible: there is no immune compartment, so on recovery individuals flow straight back into $S$. This is appropriate for infections such as many bacterial STIs or the common cold that confer little lasting immunity.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: Writing the SIS model with $S = 1 - I$ gives $\dot{I} = \beta I(1 - I) - \gamma I$. The nonzero endemic equilibrium is
questions.explanation: Set $\dot{I} = I[\beta(1 - I) - \gamma] = 0$. The nonzero root needs $\beta(1 - I) - \gamma = 0$, so $1 - I = \gamma/\beta$ and $I^* = 1 - \gamma/\beta = 1 - 1/R_0$. This is positive only when $R_0 = \beta/\gamma > 1$.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: An SIS infection has $\beta = 0.5$ per day and $\gamma = 0.2$ per day. The endemic equilibrium fraction infected $I^* = 1 - \gamma/\beta$ is
questions.explanation: First $R_0 = \beta/\gamma = 0.5/0.2 = 2.5 > 1$, so an endemic state exists. Then $I^* = 1 - \gamma/\beta = 1 - 0.4 = 0.6$. At steady state 60% of the population is infectious at any time.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: In the SIS model, if $R_0 = \beta/\gamma < 1$ then as $t \to \infty$ the infection
options: settles to a positive endemic level
questions.explanation: When $R_0 < 1$ the only nonnegative equilibrium is $I^* = 0$, and it is stable: $\dot{I} \approx (\beta - \gamma)I < 0$ near zero. So the prevalence decays to zero and the disease is eliminated. A positive endemic level requires $R_0 > 1$.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: A disease has a mean infectious period of $5$ days and each infectious person transmits at rate $\beta = 0.8$ per day in a fully susceptible population. Its $R_0$ is
questions.explanation: With mean infectious period $1/\gamma = 5$ days, $R_0 = \beta \times \frac{1}{\gamma} = 0.8 \times 5 = 4$. Equivalently $\gamma = 1/5 = 0.2$ per day and $R_0 = 0.8/0.2 = 4$.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: The effective reproduction number when a fraction $S$ of the population is still susceptible is $R_e = R_0 S$. The epidemic is in decline (infectives falling) when
questions.explanation: Infectives change according to the sign of $R_e - 1$. When $R_e = R_0 S < 1$ each case produces fewer than one new case, so $\dot{I} < 0$ and the epidemic recedes. This happens once $S < 1/R_0$.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: Dividing the SIR equations gives $\frac{dI}{dS} = -1 + \frac{\gamma}{\beta S} = -1 + \frac{1}{R_0 S}$. Integrating this relation yields a conserved quantity that is used to derive
questions.explanation: Integrating $\frac{dI}{dS}$ gives $I + S - \frac{1}{R_0}\ln S = \text{const}$ along trajectories. Setting $I = 0$ at the start and end produces the final size relation $\ln(S_0/S_\infty) = R_0(S_0 - S_\infty)$, which determines the total fraction infected over the whole epidemic.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: Which statement correctly distinguishes the long-run behaviour of the SIR and SIS models (with $R_0 > 1$)?
options: Both approach an endemic steady state with $I > 0$
SIS has a single wave, SIR is endemic
SIR has a single epidemic wave that ends with $I \to 0$, while SIS can settle to a positive endemic level
questions.explanation: In SIR, permanent immunity depletes susceptibles, so the infection produces one wave and then dies out ($I \to 0$) with some susceptibles spared. In SIS, recovered individuals become susceptible again, replenishing $S$, so the infection can persist at the endemic level $I^* = 1 - 1/R_0$.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: An SIR epidemic has transmission rate $\beta = 0.45$ per day and recovery rate $\gamma = 0.15$ per day.
questions.solution: The mean infectious period is $1/\gamma =$ {{1}} days. The basic reproduction number is $R_0 = \beta/\gamma =$ {{2}}. Since this exceeds $1$, the disease initially {{3}}.
questions.explanation: Mean infectious period $1/\gamma = 1/0.15 = 6.67$ days. $R_0 = \beta/\gamma = 0.45/0.15 = 3 > 1$, so $\dot{I} > 0$ initially and the epidemic grows (spreads).
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: A vaccination campaign targets a disease with basic reproduction number $R_0 = 5$.
questions.solution: The herd immunity threshold is $1 - 1/R_0$, so the minimum immune fraction needed is {{1}}. Once vaccination pushes the susceptible fraction below the critical value $1/R_0 =$ {{2}}, the effective reproduction number drops below $1$ and the epidemic cannot {{3}}.
questions.explanation: Herd immunity threshold $= 1 - 1/5 = 0.8$. The critical susceptible fraction is $1/R_0 = 1/5 = 0.2$. Below it $R_e = R_0 S < 1$, so each case yields fewer than one new case and the disease cannot spread.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: Consider the SIS model $\dot{I} = \beta I(1 - I) - \gamma I$ with $\beta = 0.6$ and $\gamma = 0.24$ per day.
questions.solution: The basic reproduction number is $R_0 = \beta/\gamma =$ {{1}}. Because $R_0 > 1$, there is a positive endemic equilibrium at $I^* = 1 - 1/R_0 =$ {{2}}, and this endemic state is {{3}}.
questions.explanation: $R_0 = 0.6/0.24 = 2.5$. Endemic level $I^* = 1 - 1/R_0 = 1 - 0.4 = 0.6$. For $R_0 > 1$ the endemic equilibrium is stable while the disease-free state $I = 0$ is unstable, so prevalence settles at $I^* = 0.6$.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: An SIR epidemic has $R_0 = 4$. Track the turning point of the outbreak.
questions.solution: The number of infectives peaks when the susceptible fraction falls to $S = 1/R_0 =$ {{1}}. While $S$ is above this value the infectives are {{2}}; once $S$ drops below it, the infectives {{3}}.
questions.explanation: Peak at $S = 1/R_0 = 1/4 = 0.25$, where $\dot{I} = 0$. For $S > 0.25$, $R_e = R_0 S > 1$ so infectives rise; for $S < 0.25$, $R_e < 1$ so they fall.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: Classify the basic SIR and SIS modelling assumptions about immunity after recovery.
questions.solution: In the SIR model, recovered individuals are assumed to gain {{1}} immunity and never re-enter the susceptible pool. In the SIS model, recovery confers {{2}} immunity, so individuals can be reinfected. This difference is why only the {{3}} model can sustain a disease at a constant endemic level without births.
questions.explanation: SIR assumes permanent immunity (one pass through $R$); SIS assumes no immunity (straight back to $S$). Because SIS recycles individuals into the susceptible pool, it alone can hold a steady endemic prevalence $I^* = 1 - 1/R_0$ in this simple closed-population setting.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: A pathogen has a mean infectious period of $4$ days and transmission rate $\beta = 0.75$ per day in a fully susceptible population.
questions.solution: The recovery rate is $\gamma = 1/4 =$ {{1}} per day. The basic reproduction number is $R_0 = \beta/\gamma =$ {{2}}, and the herd immunity threshold $1 - 1/R_0$ is {{3}} (as a decimal fraction, to two places).
questions.explanation: $\gamma = 1/4 = 0.25$ per day. $R_0 = \beta/\gamma = 0.75/0.25 = 3$. Herd immunity threshold $= 1 - 1/3 = 0.6667 \approx 0.67$.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology
